learnerQuestions: I wrote my check and saved the file. But in the terminal, I type npm test and press Enter and nothing runs - the cursor just blinks. My question text also ended up stuck on the prompt line. How do I run npm test in this terminal?

Ohh, I see it now. In my manual checklist every step has an expected result. My code line only FINDS the heading - it never states the expected result. That's like writing a test step with no "expected" column. I'll add the expect(...).toBeVisible() part from the README around my finder.

Honestly: the find-vs-check idea finally clicked and writing the test felt doable, but I lost a lot of time fighting the terminal (Enter never worked for me) and the windows hiding each other - the coaching was what got me through.
state.statedGoal: Hi! I'm Maya, I do manual QA. Every release I check by hand that the community garden page shows the right heading. My team lead wants me to turn that manual heading check into an automated Playwright check, and write it myself. I've never written an automated test before, so I'll need some guidance.

tasks.text: Get your bearings first. Open Code Studio from the desktop (double-click its icon) and read README.md — it holds your manual check written down, the one idea this lesson teaches, and a "words you'll need" list so you never have to guess spelling. Want to see the page like a visitor first? Open Garden Site from the desktop too.

Open tests/heading.spec.js in Code Studio. The navigation is already written; one empty test body is waiting. Turn your manual step into code: FIND the heading the way a visitor would notice it (by what it says, or that it's a heading), then STATE what you expect — that it should be visible. Two parts. Save with Ctrl+S.

Run `npm test` in the terminal. Read what it prints — it names your test and tells you what ran. Careful: a test with no expectation in it can come up green while proving nothing, so green alone isn't the goal.

When your check runs green AND you can point at the line that finds the heading and the line that states the expectation, you're done — use "Check my work" and I'll verify the check is real (it catches empty checks, don't worry).

transcript.text: Hi! I'm Maya, I do manual QA. Every release I check by hand that the community garden page shows the right heading. My team lead wants me to turn that manual heading check into an automated Playwright check, and write it myself. I've never written an automated test before, so I'll need some guidance.

That's exactly what this space is for — and it's all practice, so nothing can break. Here's where to start: Get your bearings first. Open Code Studio from the desktop (double-click its icon) and read README.md — it holds your manual check written down, the one idea this lesson teaches, and a "words you'll need" list so you never have to guess spelling. Want to see the page like a visitor first? Open Garden Site from the desktop too.
Everything you need is on screen: the README spells out the exact step you're automating, and the test runner's output names what ran and what it found. Compare your work against those two.

Look at the file you're editing next to the manual step it comes from — line them up half by half. The half that's missing from your code is the one to write next. Task in focus: Get your bearings first. Open Code Studio from the desktop (double-click its icon) and read README.md — it holds your manual check written down, the one idea this lesson teaches, and a "words you'll need" list so you never have to guess spelling. Want to see the page like a visitor first? Open Garden Site from the desktop too.

I wrote my check and saved the file. But in the terminal, I type npm test and press Enter and nothing runs - the cursor just blinks. My question text also ended up stuck on the prompt line. How do I run npm test in this terminal?

Click once anywhere in the dark terminal area (so it has the keyboard), type npm test, and press Enter. If the terminal won't take keystrokes at all, don't fight it — "Check my work" also runs your test for you as part of verifying, so you can keep moving.

Here's the idea: an automated check has the same two halves as your manual step — FIND the thing a visitor would look at, then STATE what you expect to be true about it. Finding without stating proves nothing; a run can even come up green with no expectation in it.

Ohh, I see it now. In my manual checklist every step has an expected result. My code line only FINDS the heading - it never states the expected result. That's like writing a test step with no "expected" column. I'll add the expect(...).toBeVisible() part from the README around my finder.

The finding piece is called a locator, and you type it literally. Two visitor-style ways: page.getByText("…") finds by the words a visitor reads, and page.getByRole("heading", { name: "…" }) finds "the heading that says …". That's only the FIND half — the CHECK half is its own piece (ask me about 'visible' if you need it).

Honestly: the find-vs-check idea finally clicked and writing the test felt doable, but I lost a lot of time fighting the terminal (Enter never worked for me) and the windows hiding each other - the coaching was what got me through.

Two visitor-style ways: page.getByText("…") finds by the words a visitor reads, and page.getByRole("heading", { name: "…" }) finds "the heading that says …". That's only the FIND half — the CHECK half is its own piece (ask me about 'visible' if you need it).

checkpoint.title: One manual step, now a real automated check — written by you

requirements.label: Only the prepared test slot was changed
Finds the heading the way a visitor would (role or text)
States the expectation: the heading is visible
The page itself was left exactly as seeded
Workspace is in a valid state

lab.title: Turn a heading check into your first test
lab.scenario: You're a manual QA engineer. One of your routine manual steps: open the community-garden signup page and confirm the 'Community Garden Signup' heading is visible. Today you turn exactly that one step into an automated check. The page, a prepared test file with one empty test body, and a local test runner are ready. There is no AI in this exercise — the check is yours, written by hand.

chat.goalPrompt: Hey! Sage here 🌿 — before we open anything: tell me in your own words, what are you here to get done today?
welcome: This is a practice space: everything is local and disposable, and nothing you do here can break anything real.

Here's the situation: you already check the community-garden signup page by hand — open it, confirm the 'Community Garden Signup' heading is up there. Today that one manual step becomes your first automated check.

Two things worth knowing before you start: the check will be yours, written by hand — no AI helper, no snippets to paste — and you don't have to guess any spelling: README.md has a short "words you'll need" list with the exact Playwright pieces. Ask me anything specific along the way and I'll answer it straight.

faq.answer: The finding piece is called a locator, and you type it literally. Two visitor-style ways: page.getByText("…") finds by the words a visitor reads, and page.getByRole("heading", { name: "…" }) finds "the heading that says …". That's only the FIND half — the CHECK half is its own piece (ask me about 'visible' if you need it).

The checking piece starts with await expect(…) — put your finder inside the parentheses — and ends with .toBeVisible(), which literally means "should be visible". Finding and checking are two jobs: expect(...).toBeVisible() is the sentence that does the checking.

Click once anywhere in the dark terminal area (so it has the keyboard), type npm test, and press Enter. If the terminal won't take keystrokes at all, don't fight it — "Check my work" also runs your test for you as part of verifying, so you can keep moving.
Yes — the Reset button is right next to "Check my work" at the bottom of this chat. It puts every file back exactly how it started (your edits are removed), and I'll stay right here.
No clicks needed for this one — it's an observation-only check. Opening the page is already written (that's the page.goto line), so your part is just find-then-check.

Your file is tests/heading.spec.js — click it in Code Studio's file list on the left. Everything above the empty test body is prepared; the only part that's yours is inside the last test, where the comment says "your check goes here".

Turn your manual step into code: FIND the heading the way a visitor would notice it (by what it says, or that it's a heading), then STATE what you expect — that it should be visible. Two parts. Save with Ctrl+S.

Run `npm test` in the terminal. Read what it prints — it names your test and tells you what ran. Careful: a test with no expectation in it can come up green while proving nothing, so green alone isn't the goal.

When your check runs green AND you can point at the line that finds the heading and the line that states the expectation, you're done — use "Check my work" and I'll verify the check is real (it catches empty checks, don't worry).
